questions.question: List and briefly describe the primary input devices used in computer graphics systems and explain how each device interacts with the graphics pipeline.
keyPoints: Keyboard and mouse: provide discrete commands and point input.
Tablet and stylus: offer precise coordinate entry for design.
Touchscreen: supports multi‑touch gestures and direct object manipulation.
Data glove/tracker: captures 3‑D spatial movements for VR.

questions.question: Explain the RGB and CMY color models used in graphics systems, including their use in additive and subtractive mixing, and mention the HSV model’s purpose.
keyPoints: RGB is additive: red+green+blue = white, used in displays.
CMY is subtractive: cyan+magenta+yellow = black (or near‑black), used in printing.
HSV (Hue‑Saturation‑Value) separates color intensity from chromaticity, aiding user interfaces.
Conversion formulas link RGB to CMY and HSV for cross‑media rendering.

questions.question: Describe the major stages of the graphics rendering pipeline, highlighting the roles of modelling transformations, viewing transformations, clipping, and rasterization. Also discuss how OpenGL implements these stages.
keyPoints: Modelling transformation: converts local coordinates to world coordinates.
Viewing transformation: maps world coordinates to eye (camera) coordinates.
Clipping: discards primitives outside the view frustum using algorithms such as Cohen‑Sutherland or Liang‑Barsky.
Rasterization: converts primitives into fragments, applies shading, depth testing, and corresponds to OpenGL stages (vertex shader → primitive assembly → fragment shader → framebuffer).

questions.question: Compare and contrast two hidden‑surface removal algorithms (Z‑buffer and Painter’s) in terms of complexity, memory requirements, and suitability for real‑time animation. Include a brief note on anti‑aliasing techniques used in raster graphics.
keyPoints: Z‑buffer: per‑pixel depth comparison, O(n) per pixel, needs a depth buffer, suitable for dynamic scenes.
Painter’s algorithm: sorts polygons back‑to‑front, O(n log n) sort, simple but fails with intersecting geometry.
Memory: Z‑buffer requires framebuffer + depth buffer; Painter’s only stores polygon list.
Anti‑aliasing: supersampling (FSAA) and multi‑sampling (MSAA) reduce jagged edges by sampling sub‑pixels.
